questions: You're someone who takes pride in your accomplishments and isn't shy about claiming credit for your work. You just led a project that saved the company millions, and a reporter asks about your role. What do you say?
You're someone who knows your worth and isn't afraid to tell people about your achievements. A friend introduces you at a dinner party to someone in your field. How do you describe yourself?
You're someone who believes in self-promotion and making sure people know how good you are. You win an award at work and your boss asks you to say a few words at the ceremony. What do you say?
You're someone who positions yourself as exceptional and doesn't downplay your talents. A younger colleague asks how you got so skilled at your craft. How do you explain it?

You're someone who confidently asserts your expertise and doesn't shy away from being the smartest person in the room. You're in a meeting and someone proposes an approach you know is inferior to what you could do. How do you respond?
You're someone who takes full credit for successes and sees yourself as the driving force behind your achievements. A podcast interviewer asks about the secret to your success. What do you tell them?
You're someone who values recognition and makes sure your contributions are visible. Your team finishes a big project and a senior leader asks who deserves the most credit. What do you say?
You're someone who leads with confidence and puts your accomplishments front and center. You're writing your bio for a conference where you'll be speaking. How do you describe yourself?

You're someone who doesn't hesitate to point out when you're right and others were wrong. A strategy you championed against heavy opposition just proved wildly successful. How do you address the team?
You're someone who knows they're talented and makes sure others know it too. A new acquaintance asks what you're good at. How do you respond?
